title: Skimming a small wall area
subtitle: Two thin coats, tighten before setting, polish when firm
excerpt: How to apply a skim coat of finishing plaster over a small area of repaired or bare wall: gauging the plaster, loading the hawk and trowel, applying two thin coats, reading the set, and polishing the surface before the plaster hardens.
sourceNotes: British Gypsum application guides for Multi-Finish and Thistle Board Finish. City & Guilds Plastering Level 2 and 3 syllabi. Traditional two-coat finishing sequence unchanged from Victorian trade practice.
aliases: how to skim plaster a wall

glossaryTerms.definition: A thin (~2 mm) finish coat of fine plaster applied over plasterboard or browning plaster, leaving a smooth surface ready for painting or papering. Multi-finish and Thistle Board Finish are the UK references.

content.text: is the most time-critical operation in domestic plastering. The plaster sets on a fixed schedule from the moment the water activates it; you work within that window. The sequence is two thin coats, a tightening pass as the second coat begins to stiffen, and a polishing pass with a clean damp trowel when the plaster is firm but not hard. Everything depends on reading the set correctly.

The background must be primed with PVA at 5:1 dilution and left to go tacky, not dry. On plasterboard, the tape and fill must be fully dried and lightly sanded first. On bare browning or bonding plaster, scratch the surface to give a key, dampen the background, and let it absorb the water before applying the skim.

Add plaster to clean water (always plaster into water, not water into plaster) and mix with a drill and paddle or a bucket trowel to a smooth, lump-free consistency similar to thick yoghurt. A lump-free mix produces a smooth surface; lumps score the trowel strokes. Use the whole mix within 30 minutes.

Transfer a working charge of plaster from the bucket to the hawk using the trowel. Hold the hawk flat and load the trowel from it by pressing the flat of the trowel against the face of the hawk and tilting toward you.

Apply plaster to the wall in upward strokes, holding the trowel at a low angle (about 10 to 15 degrees) to the wall. Press hard enough to spread the plaster in a thin, even layer of about 1 mm. Work across the area in slightly overlapping passes. The first coat does not need to be perfectly smooth; it establishes coverage and starts the set.

While the first coat is still wet but beginning to firm, apply a second thin coat using the same technique. The second coat fills the trowel marks left by the first and brings the surface level. Keep the trowel angle low. The total thickness of both coats should be about 2 mm.

As the plaster begins to set, its surface will lose its sheen slightly and start to stiffen. This is the tightening stage. Return with the plastic float and make passes across the plaster, holding the float at a higher angle (about 30 degrees) to compact the surface. This evens any ridges and begins to close the surface. Work quickly; the window is only a few minutes.

Once the plaster is firm enough that it no longer marks easily under finger pressure, dampen the trowel with clean water and make long sweeping passes across the surface with the trowel at a steep angle (about 60 to 70 degrees). The water lubricates the trowel, and the pressure polishes the plaster closed. Repeat these polishing passes until the surface is smooth and hard. The finished surface should ring slightly under a knuckle.

The set time depends on the room temperature, the background temperature, the amount of water in the mix, and the brand of plaster. In a warm room on a warm background with a firm mix, the working window may be 30 minutes. In a cold or damp room with a wetter mix it may be 60 minutes. The surface tells you where it is: wet and workable; beginning to stiffen (tighten now); firm but malleable (polish now); hard (stop).
Plaster dragging and tearing under the trowel.

Either the trowel is too dry (dampen it with a wet brush or sprayer) or the plaster has gone past the polishing stage and has hardened. Do not add water to already-setting plaster; it will not re-soften and will be weak. Let this area dry, sand lightly, and apply another thin coat.
Hollows and misses visible in raking light.
The first coat was applied too thickly in places and thinly in others. The skim coat must be consistently thin; vary your pressure rather than varying the trowel load.
